preFix.title: Ox gall as a surfactant in marbling
preFix.subtitle: How ox gall reduces surface tension in marbling paint and why the amount matters
preFix.excerpt: Marbling acrylic dropped onto carrageenan size without a surfactant beads up or fails to spread. Ox gall, added drop by drop to each colour, reduces the paint's surface tension enough to allow it to float and expand. Too much gall causes the colour to spread invisibly thin; too little causes it to clump.
preFix.sourceNotes: Charles W. Woolnough, The Art of Marbling (1881), chapter on colour preparation including the role of ox gall. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/artofmarbling00wool. Woolnough describes the gall as the essential agent for spreading colour on the size and specifies testing by dropping onto the tray surface.

content.text: Marbling depends on pigments floating on the surface of a thickened size rather than sinking into it. Acrylic paint out of the tube is too dense and too high in
to spread on carrageenan size. A few drops of ox gall, a natural

, reduces this tension. The colour then spreads across the size as a thin film when it makes contact, pushing adjacent colours aside to fill the available surface. Ox gall is the traditional material and remains the most reliable; synthetic surfactants such as dilute washing-up liquid work but are less controllable because concentration varies between products.
Why each colour needs individual adjustment

Different colours need different amounts of ox gall. Dark pigments (black, dark blue) are typically denser and need more gall than pale colours (yellow, light grey). The pigment load of the paint also matters: a heavily pigmented black may need three times as much gall as a lightly pigmented raw sienna. This means each colour pot must be tested and adjusted individually before laying a print.
Load an eyedropper with the thinned colour and allow one drop to fall from approximately 15 cm onto the size surface.

Watch what happens. A well-adjusted colour spreads to a circle approximately 5 to 8 cm across within two seconds and holds that diameter. A colour with too little gall beads up into a small dome less than 2 cm across and may sink. A colour with too much gall spreads beyond 15 cm in a second or two, leaving the colour too thin to be visible.
If the colour spreads too slowly or beads, add one drop of ox gall to the pot, stir, and test again. Repeat until the drop spreads correctly.
If the colour spreads too fast, thin it further with water (which reduces pigment concentration and therefore density), then retest.
Skim the test drops from the size with a newspaper strip before laying the first print.

Each colour dropped onto the size pushes all existing colours outward. The first colour dropped occupies the most surface area; each subsequent colour pushes the previous ones into smaller, thinner zones. In practice this means the first colour dropped should have slightly more gall than the last, since it must spread against nothing, while the last colour is spreading against a surface already fully covered. Woolnough noted that experienced marblers adjust gall levels sequentially, adding a fraction more to each pot in the laying order.
